content:
I start to think about how to write a simple version of the quote (or we can include it inside kproc-macros? but it looks too big for our case)!

This is a Makefile syntax proposal that I would like to discuss https://github.com/vincenzopalazzo/kproc-macros/issues/5 also do not make the grammar ambiguous.

In addition I released a new alpha version https://crates.io/crates/kproc-parser so if want help in find some bug, please do!

This is an example of how to use the library https://github.com/vincenzopalazzo/kproc-macros/blob/main/kproc-examples/src/main.rs and this is an example of how to parser a token stream with KTokenStream https://github.com/vincenzopalazzo/kproc-macros/blob/main/kproc-macros-examples/src/lib.rs

P.S: it may required nightly due the error reporting feature, suggestion on how to improve it are welcome

I think your proposal is actually more complicated than quote. It needs a proc macro to be implemented while quote is a decl macro. In addition something like get_${attributes[0].name} being a single identifier is not implemented at all as it parsed the same as get_ ${attributes[0].name} which would be two identifiers. As such proc macros can't distinguish between the two.

Quote's syntax is really simple. It only has #var for taking a thing stored in the variable var and converting it into a token tree before pasting it and it had #()* for repetition. Both mirror the decl macro syntax, except replacing $ with #.

Sure, quote's actual implementation is conplicated, but that is necessary to prevent exponential runtime in terms of input size. (rustc's decl macro expansion has some cases where it runs in exponential time. quote avoids these cases.)

bjorn3 said:

I think your proposal is actually more complicated than quote. It needs a proc macro to be implemented while quote is a decl macro. In addition something like get_${attributes[0].name} being a single identifier is not implemented at all as it parsed the same as get_ ${attributes[0].name} which would be two identifiers. As such proc macros can't distinguish between the two.

In addition something like get_${attributes[0].name} being a single identifier is not implemented at all as it parsed the same as get_ ${attributes[0].name} which would be two identifiers.

I did not understand it! can make an example?

I think your proposal is actually more complicated than quote.

Do not put my assertion in the wrong context! My propose is simple than quote to integrate inside the kernel because it do not contains proc_macro2. If the syntaxt or not is complex is another problem.

bjorn3 said:

Quote's syntax is really simple. It only has #var for taking a thing stored in the variable var and converting it into a token tree before pasting it and it had #()* for repetition. Both mirror the decl macro syntax, except replacing $ with #.

I found the Makefile syntax more readable than the quote one! from me that I read with a screen reader this syntax is shit because the screen reader did not know how to read #()*. But yeah of curse it works for most of people so who cares

bjorn3 said:

Sure, quote's actual implementation is conplicated, but that is necessary to prevent exponential runtime in terms of input size. (rustc's decl macro expansion has some cases where it runs in exponential time. quote avoids these cases.)

this not exclude that it is possible do the same optimizzation in alternative of quote.

Vincenzo Palazzo (vincenzopalazzo) said:

bjorn3 said:

I think your proposal is actually more complicated than quote. It needs a proc macro to be implemented while quote is a decl macro. In addition something like get_${attributes[0].name} being a single identifier is not implemented at all as it parsed the same as get_ ${attributes[0].name} which would be two identifiers. As such proc macros can't distinguish between the two.

In addition something like get_${attributes[0].name} being a single identifier is not implemented at all as it parsed the same as get_ ${attributes[0].name} which would be two identifiers.

I did not understand it! can make an example?

Ah ok, I get it! this is the same of get_#var or this include some special fact that I did not know?
Yeah, get_#var doesn't work either. A macro sees get_ # var.

bjorn3 said:

Yeah, get_#var doesn't work either. A macro sees get_ # var.

well I think this is a macros parser problem ad this point I guess, maybe we ca discuss how how we can make this kind of syntax without all this edge case?

I really like the option to have @foreach ....

and maybe if you can make me in contact with some procedural macros team member I would really like to discuss. I think the error message api that are in experimental mode are bad and it is possible do better
but I faild to find this kind of people
Making get_#var work requires changing the lexer, as well as breaking existing macros (if it isn't introduced as entirely new api).
Did you voice your concerns about the proc macro diagnostics api at https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/54140 or elsewhere?

bjorn3 said:

Did you voice your concerns about the proc macro diagnostics api at https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/54140 or elsewhere?

no one is tracking this issue, I'm find the guys that was working on it as last and analyze why the error are in this way

bjorn3 said:

Making get_#var work requires changing the lexer, as well as breaking existing macros (if it isn't introduced as entirely new api).

I do not think this is the only way, you can mark the parser with a top level feature and tell the parser to include the space, if this is top level feature is not specified the parser keep emit the same token stream of now!

This is not a breaking change and you can break the change later with a new rust edition

In addition
bjorn3 said:

Making get_#var work requires changing the lexer, as well as breaking existing macros (if it isn't introduced as entirely new api).

in addition I think the space here ti is not a conern, practcal concern, you could do yes, but in the most of the case you are producing wrong rust code and the compiler will output the error.

I do not know if you have a valid example when writing a proc macros with additional space can output a valid rust code and change the meaning of what the user want to write.

Regarding how strange the error of the compiler is, we could work on the quality of error message later, without over engignering too much the problem, because overn engigneering the problem is most of the time the killer

bjorn3 said:

Yeah, get_#var doesn't work either. A macro sees get_ # var.

Could a proc macro check the source locations?
The span api is unstable and in addition it breaks if the quote! invocation is inside a macro expansion itself as Span would point to the outer macro expansion.
@Gary Guo maybe we can consider to rework this idea to make a paste + quote? https://github.com/vincenzopalazzo/kproc-macros/issues/5 this will be useful when a user will write a proc macros
